reviewQuestions.question: Why must the same analyzer run at index time and query time?
reviewQuestions.answer: Search matches tokens, not raw text. If indexing lowercases and stems but the query does not, the query token will never equal the indexed token, so the document is unfindable.

reviewQuestions.question: How does an inverted index differ from a forward index, and why is it faster for search?
reviewQuestions.answer: A forward index maps doc → terms; an inverted index maps term → docs (postings). Search asks 'which docs contain this term', which the inverted index answers directly by reading one posting list instead of scanning every document.

reviewQuestions.question: What does the IDF factor accomplish in TF-IDF?
reviewQuestions.answer: IDF down-weights terms that appear in many documents (like 'the') and up-weights rare terms, because rare terms are more discriminative for relevance.

reviewQuestions.question: Why does BM25 normalize by document length?
reviewQuestions.answer: Without length normalization a long document accumulates term matches just by being long. The b parameter scales the score by document length relative to the average so long and short documents compete fairly.

reviewQuestions.question: How is BM25 different from TF-IDF?
reviewQuestions.answer: BM25 adds two things TF-IDF lacks: term-frequency saturation (the k1 parameter, so the 10th occurrence adds little) and document-length normalization (the b parameter). It is TF-IDF with diminishing returns and fairness built in.

reviewQuestions.question: When can BM25 outperform embedding-based retrieval?
reviewQuestions.answer: On exact terms, rare tokens, codes, names, and identifiers — anything where the literal string matters. Embeddings capture meaning but blur exact tokens, so BM25 wins on precision for keyword-literal queries.

reviewQuestions.question: How would you combine BM25 and vector search?
reviewQuestions.answer: Run both retrievers, then fuse their rankings — typically with reciprocal rank fusion, which uses ranks (not incomparable scores). Validate that the fused result beats both single retrievers on an eval set.

reviewQuestions.question: When would you use MRR versus nDCG?
reviewQuestions.answer: MRR rewards getting one correct answer near the top — good for known-item search. nDCG uses graded relevance discounted by rank — good when there are multiple results of varying usefulness.

reviewQuestions.question: Why must the eval query set be frozen before tuning?
reviewQuestions.answer: If you change the eval set while tuning, you are optimizing to a moving target and can no longer tell whether a change is a real improvement or a regression. A frozen set makes comparisons valid.

reviewQuestions.question: Why use reciprocal rank fusion instead of averaging BM25 and cosine scores?
reviewQuestions.answer: BM25 and cosine scores live on different, uncalibrated scales — averaging them is meaningless. RRF uses each result's rank position, which is comparable across retrievers.

reviewQuestions.question: Why is reranking done as a second stage instead of over the whole corpus?
reviewQuestions.answer: Cross-encoder rerankers are accurate but expensive — too slow to run over millions of documents. A cheap retriever narrows to ~100 candidates; the reranker only reorders those, getting precision within a latency budget.

reviewQuestions.question: What does 'distance' mean in an embedding space?
reviewQuestions.answer: An embedding model places semantically similar content close together, so geometric distance approximates semantic dissimilarity. Search becomes finding the nearest vectors to the query vector.

reviewQuestions.question: When do cosine similarity and dot product give the same ranking?
reviewQuestions.answer: When all vectors are L2-normalized to unit length: then dot product equals cosine, so they rank identically. On un-normalized vectors, dot product is also influenced by magnitude.

reviewQuestions.question: How do you return top-k without sorting all n scores?
reviewQuestions.answer: Maintain a size-k min-heap. For each score, if the heap has fewer than k items push it; otherwise if it beats the heap root, replace the root. This is O(n log k) time and O(k) space.

reviewQuestions.question: Why is a brute-force index essential even though it is slow?
reviewQuestions.answer: It is exact — recall is 1.0 by definition. It is the ground-truth oracle against which an approximate index's recall is measured. Without it, ANN recall is unknowable.

reviewQuestions.question: What do the layers in an HNSW graph do?
reviewQuestions.answer: Upper layers are sparse with long-range links for fast coarse navigation; lower layers are dense for fine-grained search. A query takes long hops up top, then descends to refine — turning O(n) scan into O(log n) traversal.

reviewQuestions.question: What does the efSearch parameter trade off in HNSW?
reviewQuestions.answer: efSearch is the size of the dynamic candidate list during search. Larger efSearch explores more of the graph: higher recall but higher latency. It is the main recall/latency knob at query time.

reviewQuestions.question: What happens to recall and latency as you increase nprobe in IVF?
reviewQuestions.answer: nprobe is how many Voronoi cells a query scans. More cells means more vectors examined: higher recall, higher latency. nprobe=1 is fast but misses neighbours near cell boundaries.

reviewQuestions.question: How does product quantization shrink vector memory?
reviewQuestions.answer: It splits each vector into m sub-vectors and replaces each with the 1-byte id of its nearest centroid in a per-sub-space codebook. A 1536-d float32 vector (6KB) becomes a few bytes, at the cost of approximate distances.

reviewQuestions.question: Compare pre-filtering and post-filtering in vector search.
reviewQuestions.answer: Pre-filtering restricts the candidate set before search — exact, but can be slow inside a graph index. Post-filtering runs ANN then drops non-matches — fast, but may return fewer than k results when matches are sparse.

reviewQuestions.question: Why report a recall/latency curve instead of a single recall number?
reviewQuestions.answer: Recall alone is meaningless without its cost. Every ANN knob trades recall for latency or memory, so the honest artifact is a curve showing the achievable operating points, anchored by brute force at recall 1.0.

reviewQuestions.question: Why is retrieval the quality ceiling of a RAG system?
reviewQuestions.answer: The model can only answer from the context it receives. If retrieval misses the relevant chunk, no amount of model quality recovers the answer — so most 'RAG is bad' problems are retrieval problems.

reviewQuestions.question: When would you choose RAG over fine-tuning?
reviewQuestions.answer: RAG when knowledge changes often, must be cited, or is too large to bake into weights. Fine-tuning when you need to change behavior, format, or style rather than inject facts. They are often combined.

reviewQuestions.question: What is the tradeoff in choosing chunk size?
reviewQuestions.answer: Large chunks dilute embeddings and retrieve irrelevant text alongside the answer; small chunks lose surrounding context and may split an answer. Good chunking respects structure and adds overlap.

reviewQuestions.question: What is the 'lost in the middle' effect and how do you mitigate it?
reviewQuestions.answer: LLMs attend more strongly to the start and end of the context window than the middle. Mitigate by placing the strongest evidence at the edges, deduping, and cutting low-value context rather than overflowing the window.

reviewQuestions.question: Why constrain LLM output to a schema instead of parsing prose?
reviewQuestions.answer: Prose is unreliable to parse and breaks silently. Schema-constrained generation yields a typed object your code can validate and trust — or explicitly reject — making the LLM-to-code boundary safe.

reviewQuestions.question: In tool calling, what does the LLM actually do — and not do?
reviewQuestions.answer: The LLM decides which tool to call and with what arguments (it is the planner). It does not execute anything — your code runs the tool and feeds the result back into context.

reviewQuestions.question: What guardrails keep an agent loop from running away?
reviewQuestions.answer: A hard step/iteration limit, a bounded context (summarize or drop old turns), explicit stopping conditions, and a human-approval gate before irreversible actions.

reviewQuestions.question: Why must model routing be backed by evals?
reviewQuestions.answer: Routing sends some requests to a weaker model to save cost. Without evals you cannot tell whether that routed traffic still meets quality — cost savings would silently come at the price of regressions.

reviewQuestions.question: Why must an LLM-as-judge be validated against human labels?
reviewQuestions.answer: An LLM judge has its own biases and blind spots. If it disagrees with humans, your eval scores are wrong. Validate it on a labelled sample before trusting it to gate releases.

reviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between HTTP 401 and 403?
reviewQuestions.answer: 401 Unauthorized means the request is not authenticated — you have not proven who you are. 403 Forbidden means you are authenticated but not allowed to perform this action.

reviewQuestions.question: How should API keys be stored, and why?
reviewQuestions.answer: Store only a hash of the key, like a password — so a database leak does not expose usable keys. Show the secret once at creation, add a searchable prefix, and support rotation and revocation.

reviewQuestions.question: How does a token bucket differ from a fixed-window counter?
reviewQuestions.answer: A fixed window resets counts on a boundary, allowing up to 2x the limit across the boundary. A token bucket refills continuously, smoothing the rate while still permitting bursts up to the bucket size.

reviewQuestions.question: Why are idempotency keys needed for POST endpoints?
reviewQuestions.answer: Network failures make clients retry, and retrying a non-idempotent POST (like a payment) repeats the effect. An idempotency key lets the server recognize a duplicate and replay the original result instead of re-executing.

reviewQuestions.question: What problem does a dead-letter queue solve?
reviewQuestions.answer: A message that always fails (a poison message) would be retried forever, blocking the queue. A DLQ moves it aside after N attempts so the pipeline keeps flowing and the failure can be inspected.

reviewQuestions.question: Why add jitter to retry backoff?
reviewQuestions.answer: Without jitter, many clients that failed at the same moment retry at the same moment, creating a synchronized thundering herd. Randomized delay spreads the retries out.

reviewQuestions.question: What is a cache stampede and how do you prevent it?
reviewQuestions.answer: When a hot key expires, many concurrent requests all miss and hit the origin at once. Prevent it with a lock/single-flight so only one request recomputes, or with early/probabilistic refresh before expiry.

reviewQuestions.question: What question does each of metrics, traces, and logs answer?
reviewQuestions.answer: Metrics tell you that something is wrong; traces tell you where in the system; logs tell you why. A correlation id stitches them into one story for a request.

reviewQuestions.question: Why is 'exactly-once' delivery usually a myth, and what do you do instead?
reviewQuestions.answer: Failures and retries make true exactly-once delivery effectively impossible end-to-end. Design for at-least-once delivery plus idempotent consumers, which yields exactly-once *effects*.

reviewQuestions.question: Why should every list endpoint be paginated from day one?
reviewQuestions.answer: An unpaginated list grows without bound and eventually returns huge responses or times out. Adding pagination later is a breaking change, so it should be designed in from the start (cursor-based preferred).

reviewQuestions.question: What is the core tradeoff between JWTs and server sessions?
reviewQuestions.answer: JWTs are stateless and fast to verify but hard to revoke before expiry. Sessions are revocable instantly but require a lookup store. Choose based on how quickly you must be able to revoke access.

reviewQuestions.question: When would you choose an LSM tree over a B-tree?
reviewQuestions.answer: Choose an LSM tree for write-heavy workloads: it turns random writes into sequential ones. Choose a B-tree for read-heavy workloads with in-place updates and predictable read latency.

reviewQuestions.question: Why are B-tree nodes sized to disk pages?
reviewQuestions.answer: Disk and memory move data in pages. Sizing a node to a page means one IO loads many keys, keeping the tree shallow (~3-4 levels) so a lookup costs only a few IOs even for billions of rows.

reviewQuestions.question: Why can reads be slow in an LSM tree, and what helps?
reviewQuestions.answer: A key may live in the memtable or any SSTable, so a read might check several files (read amplification). Bloom filters let a read skip files that definitely lack the key; compaction reduces the file count.

reviewQuestions.question: Why write to a WAL before updating the main data structure?
reviewQuestions.answer: The main structure may be mid-update when a crash hits, leaving it corrupt. An append-only WAL records the intended change durably first, so recovery can replay it and restore a consistent state.

reviewQuestions.question: Why is fsync required for WAL durability?
reviewQuestions.answer: A write() syscall only hands data to the OS page cache, which can be lost on a crash. fsync forces it to stable storage. The write may only be acknowledged to the client after the WAL entry is fsync'd.

reviewQuestions.question: What does compaction cost, and what does it buy?
reviewQuestions.answer: Compaction rewrites data (write amplification, IO spikes) but it reclaims space from tombstones/overwrites and bounds read amplification by reducing the number of files a read must check.

reviewQuestions.question: Why can't you treat object storage like a local disk?
reviewQuestions.answer: Object storage has ~tens-of-ms latency, per-request billing, and no in-place edits. Systems built on it batch data into large immutable segment files and keep a hot cache in front to hide the latency.

reviewQuestions.question: Why split a search platform into an indexing path and a query path?
reviewQuestions.answer: They have opposite requirements: indexing is durable, batchy, and write-optimized; querying is low-latency, cacheable, and read-optimized. Separating them lets each be designed and scaled independently.

reviewQuestions.question: What are the two pipelines in a RAG system, and what binds them?
reviewQuestions.answer: An offline ingestion pipeline (chunk → embed → index) and an online query pipeline (retrieve → rerank → pack → generate). An eval harness binds them — it gates every change to either pipeline.
